speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Genie Glucksman. Today's date is March 10, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Fritz-- Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. I'm conducting the interview in Rockville, Maryland, USA, and the language of the interview is English. My name is Genie Glucksman. Today's date is March 10, 1997. I'm conducting an interview today with Dr. Fritz Gluckstein. I'm conducting the interview in Rockville, Maryland, USA, and the language of the interview is English. I'd like to thank you for agreeing to do the interview. Could you tell me what your name is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fritz Gluckstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-R-I-T-Z G-L-U-C-K-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the name you were givenat birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- at birth was Fritz Gluckstein, an umlaut over the U.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names, middle names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name is Fritz Paul Julius Herman. And at one time, it was Israel, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you use those names for any purposes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not used. Only the name Israel had to be used later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll talk about that later. When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 24, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you currently?

speaker: interviewee
text: 70 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Berlin, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your household when you were a young boy growing up? Who were the members of your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and we had a maid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frieda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she a maid of longstanding? Had she been with the family a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she came when I was-- I still remember her coming. I must have been three years old. I remember just, even remember her dress. But about three years old, I would imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did she remain with the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: She remained, I would say, til 1933, when Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she forced to leave--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that position?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at that time-- number one, my father lost his job, and we couldn't afford a maid. And number two, the Nuremberg Laws prohibited the employment of any help under the age of 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe-- first, tell me your father's name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Georg. George without an E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the same last name as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a judge. Actually, very-- he'd give a somewhat stern appearance, particularly whenever you wanted to take a picture of him. He immediately takes a position. And of course, I have that from him. But he was a judge, but he wasn't judgmental. Had a great sense of humor. And actually, I enjoyed being with him. I enjoyed meeting his colleagues. I grew among lawyers, and I still remember-- our birthdays are very close together. He was born on the 27th of January. And I remember, I had my birthday, and then, a few days later, his birthday. And I never could figure out, grown men, they sit in a big room, smoke cigars, drink something. There are no games. I could come in and say good night, and hello, but actually, it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I still remember one of my father's colleague, who didn't-- when you met him, he didn't say hello, how are you. He said, what do you have to say in your defense? I still remember it. And Jewish lawyers, non-Jewish lawyers, a few engineers, too, but primarily lawyers. And what sort of things did you like to do with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we used to go on hikes. And my father had a map, and he showed me exactly, here we are on the map. And I still remember, my father was actually very-- he never would be seen without a coat. But on rare occasions, like [INAUDIBLE] did our hikes, he really didn't have a coat. He even-- I remember my father, the judge-- well, we couldn't-- there was a fence, and a hole in the fence, and he actually slipped through the fence. It impressed me. So he had the sense of humor, and the when necessary, he knew how to unbend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he take an active part in your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from the beginning, and later on in school. Every time I came home, every night, now, what was going on. What was covered. Were you asked, did you ever recite, what did you answer. Any tests, or so on. And I remember later on, I might go ahead, it was-- I didn't do a good job in mathematics, it was a bad test.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lo and behold, we went to the synagogue that night, and who was sitting in front of us? My math teacher. It occurred to me now, I told my father there was no test, so that he didn't know about it. Now, what would happen if he were to ask the mathematics teacher, well, how did he do? I've got to say, of course, that it was Shabbat, amd it wasn't discussed, and of course nothing happened. No, it was very important. He always kept that. Did you eat meals together as a family? Dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but if I recall, regularly my father was at several courts at the same time. The [? liebercourt ?]. And very often he came home late. But I remember some meals, but not regularly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very. But he was a religious man, absolutely. I was raised Jewish from the first day on, and we went to synagogue regularly. We made the kiddush. Usually a candle were lit, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the blessings. In fact, whenever I hear the blessings now, I still hear them in German like my father did it. [? But ?] frequently in synagogues, you see in Berlin some different setup. There's one-- there was one overall congregation, as such. And within the congregation we had the conservative, the liberal, and the reform. Conservatives would see what we have, perhaps a little bit more orthodox than the conservative here. The liberal was in between the reform and conservative. There you had an organ, but women and men are separated, except one synagogue where they are together. And then you had the reform. No head covering, and services was primarily in German. But he actually didn't belong to synagogue. Usually you went to the synagogue closest to you, but of course, the rabbis, each synagogue had a rabbi. But the rabbis took turns. Once a month, they went elsewhere. The same with the cantors. Each synagogue had actually two cantors, and they took turns, too. I remember my father selecting the rabbis that he liked to hear. And I remember some sermons I enjoyed. Some I didn't understand. And some I was bored to tears. I remember one rabbi, it was Dr. [? Weiner. ?] He was a field rabbi during the war. My father knew him from the war. And he was pretty good, but he used the word noteworthy, or the equivalent in German, and I was sitting there and counting, and I told him today you used it 6 times, or 7 times. But we enjoyed going to the synagogue. I enjoyed the singing. Usually he went to liberal, but occasionally my father went to orthodox. I would say conservative. The orthodox kept themself apart. They actually belong to the congregation, but they kept themself apart. They had their synagogue in a certain part of town, and the Orthodox Jews lived close by. They called them orthodox, we considered them ultra orthodox. What conservative, we considered orthodox, but this are semantics.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of some of the rabbis that you would attend their services--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but of course over the years-- when-- I was young, but. Yes, I remember the famous Rabbi Nussbaum. He was-- he had certain mannerisms, but he was very dramatic. He came to the United States. Then there was the Rabbi Prince. He had one-- in his synagogue, men and women sat together. And there was one rabbi who-- well, my father didn't like him because the sermons were impossible. However, he was an excellent administrator. He administered the old people's home, and that was his forte.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zalamunski. ?] And then of course, I remember the rabbi for my bar mitzvah. [? Oberlander. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: He officiated at your bar mitzvah? [? FG: Oberlander, ?] yes. Did you ever have occasion to attend a service where the Rabbi Leo Baeck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In fact, I remember one certain service and in fact I went-- at the wedding of the Rabbi [? Oberlander, ?] it was performed by Baeck. Baeck-- this I heard from my father-- was a very learned rabbi. His sermons were quite-- many people didn't understand what he was. I remember, I didn't quite follow him, either. But he was highly respected. There was no chief rabbi in Berlin. It didn't exist. But he was the chief rabbi, actually. He was quite-- his personality. In fact, you could feel when you saw him, he was quite-- you could see that he was very distinct. Even I could, as a little boy, could see. Probably feel it, how he was treated. With reverence. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the synagogue, did sit with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Absolutely. And I learned quite the liturgy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he point out things to you, and explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. You see the [INAUDIBLE] prayer book, Hebrew one side, German on the other, and yes, he pointed out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a formal Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went-- while I was in grade school, even after the takeover by the Nazis. We had religious instruction, and then later on, I went regularly-- I don't recall, once or twice a week to [INAUDIBLE] school during the day. The afternoons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of subjects would you study in your religious instruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: History. At one time, I knew all the kings of Judah and Israel. I knew all by heart. No longer. And Hebrew, too. Unfortunately, probably have forgotten more Hebrew than I ever knew. But an effort was made to teach us Hebrew at the beginning. I remember the rabbi who taught us. And then once he said, who can tell me how do you pronounce [HEBREW], not [HEBREW]. And I knew that, and he gave me a dime for this, I remember it. It was quite something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Probably not very good, but not bad, either. But to be perfectly honest, we didn't like to go particularly to afternoons. Go to religious school, not be [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did it work. Did you have to go to a different school, or did the instructor come to your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened in the public school. At the beginning they came to our school. Then we had to go to another public school close by, they combined. Let's say there were three in the area-- precinct with three public schools, and all the Jewish student met at a particular school for the religious class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was during regular class hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- [INAUDIBLE] time was taken-- we were given time. Even at the beginning, we actually took part at the Christian instruction. I don't know how that happened, but I remember he was very interesting. But beyond that, that was it. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took part in that because it just happened to work out that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It just happened. I believe at that reason, either the teacher didn't come or it wasn't set up yet. It happened to be they had religious instruction, [INAUDIBLE] good. You didn't wander around in German school out in the hall. Unheard of. Therefore you'd just [? chatter. ?] And I found it quite interesting, what he had to say. But beyond that, it was mere curiosity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any fuss or difficulty in having the Jewish students have some special treatment? Go to a special religion instructor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, this was given.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many other Jewish students would you say--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later. And I would say there were about four or five or even six in the class.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to an all boys--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But that's, of course, after. In fact, I went to school after the take overby the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to come back to that, but before I do, I'd like to ask you about your mother. Could you tell us her name and describe your mother for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hedwig. H-E-D-W-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heinrich. H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe her appearance?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she was a very beautiful woman. I remember she had very long hair. Very long hair, and wore it in a bun. And I've been told that she didn't like it at that time. it was old fashioned, you know. But my father insisted. However, she got an ally and a good friend. He was a doctor. His name was [? Hillovkoski. ?] Later on he was the chief of gynecology at the Jewish hospital. And he said, well, for health reasons, the hair is too heavy, OK, and therefore, they had to take it off. Now remember, the day my mother came with short hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think of her short hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father think of her short hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was outvoted. I don't know exactly what happened with the [INAUDIBLE]. I was told afterwards that he was quite against it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother and father meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father one time went down one of the main thoroughfares in Berlin and passed a bank, with a young lady trying to get the marquee up and down, it was stuck. He helped her, let me help you, and that's the way they met.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with that act of chivalry, he met your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excellent, that's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was their courtship like? Do you know? Did they tell you stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they don't. I know that, of course, some eyebrows were raised for the families, but only temporarily. And I must--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me why some eyebrows were raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, usually marrying a Jewish person-- not to be ultra anti-Semitic, but even there, Protestant married Protestant, Catholic married Catholics, and Jews married Jews. What's the order there?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was not a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was Protestant. Lutheran, what they call in Germany they have it either Catholic or Evangelical, Evangelisch, that's what they call it, but in effect it's Lutheran. But of course I was told I guess my father's side, particularly Aunt Bertha who was my father's aunt, Was somewhat surprised too, but after she met my mother she was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your aunt Bertha sort of the female head of the household?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually yes, one of my father's cousin, not many relatives left, but she was, Aunt Bertha, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Baumgard. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so eventually she--

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I was being told when I was born one uncle had to go out, get a big cab, to she was very particular, didn't like to go by car, the biggest cab, and she rode down to the hospital to visit my mother. On the other side, of course, even if eyebrows were raised, my father was a judge. And that was quite-- at that time a judge was a very good position and therefore. But the way I understand the eyebrow raising was only temporary, and everything worked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did this mixed marriage sit with your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite well. And once, wondered perhaps in the beginning. But they were very nice, and in fact, I can't complain, I got Christmas presents. We had actually a Christmas tree at Christmas time. And of course I had friends and non-Jewish friends and relatives who went to Christmas parties. It was very fine. And we occasionally went to church. I found it very interesting, you could watch the organist. And while there was no cantor, there was singing. And I actually enjoyed going.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in awhile. Occasionally at Christmas, very nice, even now I like to go to Christmas service. And the sermon I don't recall too much, but I enjoy a sermon. A good sermon any denomination, even now I enjoy it. I remember the big church was quite-- people singing-- something different, but of course the organist really fascinated me. They have an organ in the liberal Synagogue too but you couldn't see it, but I could stand right next to organist and watch him. And I was seeing the number of songs that posted, you see, and everybody knew. It was quite interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your mother as a religious person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't religious. But she went to church occasionally, but [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she probably would have converted, but my father said he didn't want it. You were raised, born Lutheran. I don't want you to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents get married? Did they get married in a civil ceremony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually in Germany, you have to have a civil ceremony. That is what counts. Legal is civil. But afterwords you can go to religious ceremony, but you have to have a civil ceremony.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents between themselves decide how you would be raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but at that time my father was the head of the family, he presented the family, therefore the children were to be raised Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is what was done?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how it was done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you circumscribed-- circumcised? Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I remember what happened, were several physicians present. And I was told one of the physicians went home, and his wife thought he was elsewhere and shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mistakenly, I hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mistaken. Yes certainly, you could tell he was out of breath. The woman was disturbed, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- excuse me. Now when you started at school, you went to a secular school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, when I was six years old, 1933, I entered the neighborhood school. You more or less had to go there. If you wanted to go elsewhere, you had special permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you really started school-- by the time you started school, Hitler was chancellor, and had they started enacting any anti-Jewish regulations?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933, [INAUDIBLE] my father, I remember when I went to school, my father still had the nice apartment. I got my first report card still in the nice apartment, then we had to move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you say the nice apartment, could you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: First we lived [COUGH] in a very nice apartment. I remember, I was six years old. But it didn't have hot water, and it didn't have central heating. Of course, that wouldn't do, and we moved to a very nice apartment in a different part of town which had all those conveniences. I remember of course, even had a maid there. But it was very close. I remember actually we were out, on a-- we went out summer, took an apartment in the suburbs away at the lakes. And I remember vaguely where father got the notice that he was dismissed. I remember that vaguely. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: So quite early on the rise of Hitler had an impact on your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not right when he came to power. It was January, but quite early. When I went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this apartment, this nice apartment that you had just moved into, was it in a Jewish quarter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not at all, no. Actually there were very few, I would see the Western part-- let's put it this way. The Western part of Berlin had the upper and middle class Jews, the central eastern part had the blue collar worker and the people that had immigrated from the east. But there was no ghetto as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were living in the upper middle class quarter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say yes. Not the really, there was some actually where they had houses called villa, as we have here, but still apartment. In order to live in a house, you were considered quite affluent. But we had a very nice apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have your own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unfortunately not.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have constituted a very nice apartment, how many rooms would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, very simple. A study or gentleman's room, living room, more or less [INAUDIBLE] living room or eating room, then bedroom, room for the children, and a room for the maid. That was usually-- some had more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had indoor plumbing in each apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your neighbors-- did you have any non-Jewish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I recall, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your relations with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very nice. Of course, we lived on the top floor and we made a lot of noise, and sometimes they complained, but they were very nice, and they made it very clear when we left they hated us, hated our leaving it, I remember. Even gave me candy, and it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you left, where did you-- did you move out of that quarter, out of the neighborhood, or just to a smaller apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right around the corner, [INAUDIBLE] a smaller apartment in the courtyard. You had to go through the front building into the courtyard. And fewer rooms but still very nice. And so most of my time, six years till we had to move out there I spent there. And same school, it was nice too, I could go to the same school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what were the circumstances that led to your leaving that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was later on we had to leave it. That was quite a bit-- that was actually after the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were compelled to leave it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were compelled to leave it. Or found it wise to do so, I'm quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to leave possessions behind as well, or were you able to take those with you to your new living arrangement?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't take them at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your schooling. What were your favorite subjects in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well actually, grade school I didn't have any favorite subjects. But later on in high school yes, but actually in grade school, and actually was extremely fortunate with that grade school or public school. I only can report what happened to me, but the teacher, the homeroom teacher, still his name was [? Schulze ?] a party member, the emblem right here, not once did he make an anti-Semitic remark. He didn't treat us six or seven Jewish students any different. Absolutely. I must say the man was, his behavior was impeccable. Of course the school was such, it was fortunately the principal-- his name [? was Hinzer ?]-- kept things really in order. Quite sure, of course, I can only guess, that he tried his best to keep the Nazis out. But I must say, I have only pleasant memories of school. Not once did I hear an anti-Semitic remark. Later on, however, certain students had to be admitted, and they started really quite anti-Semitic, calling names and harassment. And actually my parents went to see the principal, and he made it clear that he simply cannot dismiss, he can't expel him. At that time, I left the public school. Officially I wasn't required to do so, but the principal said I can't, I did what I-- it would be better if I would leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that it was no longer within his power to quell that kind of behavior in the school.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not-- there was no Nazi teachers because the principal more or less could determine what teacher he wanted, but the students he could not do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now to change tapes.
